heroDescription: Non-Bank RMBS Lender • A$15.9bn AUM • 63.6% Controlling Shareholder
heroCompanyDescription: Resimac Group Limited (ASX: RMC) is a non-bank residential mortgage lender with A$15.9 billion in assets under management, funded primarily through residential mortgage-backed securitisation (RMBS). Operations span home loans (~83% of AUM), asset finance, and a legacy New Zealand portfolio in run-off. Controlled by Duncan Saville (63.56% via Somers Limited), with new CEO Pete Lirantzis appointed April 2025.

skew.rationale: 60% combined weight on downside scenarios (N2 Structural Narrowing at 40% + N3 Capital Extraction at 20%). Market pricing cyclical recovery at 1.17x P/B, while evidence-weighted fair value is A$1.02. 1H26 improvement is rate-driven, not operational. Organic AUM growth 4% vs 25.3% sector.

verdict.text: Resimac is trading at A$1.17 (1.17× P/B), 12.8% above the evidence-weighted fair value of A$1.02. The market is pricing a cyclical recovery narrative where 1H26's 163% EBIT improvement marks the beginning of multi-period recovery. The evidence assigns higher probability to macro-masked structural erosion where the 1H26 inflection is the cyclical peak, not the recovery start. Organic home loan AUM grew 4% vs 25.3% sector. Management attributes NIM recovery to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement. Governance concentration (63.56% controlling shareholder chairing the Audit Committee) and ASIC civil penalty proceedings add structural risk. Verdict: WAIT. The August 2026 FY26 result is the definitive discriminator.

featuredRationale: 12.8% above fair value. 1H26 recovery rate-driven. Organic AUM growth 4% vs 25.3% sector. WAIT until August 2026.

paragraphs: Resimac just delivered its best half-year since FY22: EBIT up 163%, operating cash flow swinging A$456M positive, and the market re-rated the stock from below book value to a 17% premium in five trading days. Organic home loan growth was 4% in a sector growing at 25%. Management attributes the margin recovery to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement. The question is whether the earnings inflection marks the start of a recovery or the peak of a temporary reprieve.

For two years through mid-2024, Resimac traded at 68–77 cents per dollar of book value, a market verdict that the franchise would not earn its cost of capital. Net income had halved from its FY22 peak, the home loan book was contracting, and the CEO resigned in July 2024. Within eighteen months: new CEO appointed, A$1.4bn Westpac auto portfolio acquired, New Zealand exited, A$161M returned to shareholders at nearly five times annual earnings, ASIC civil penalty proceedings commenced, and no finalised strategy disclosed. Then three RBA rate cuts totalling 75 basis points widened net interest margin by 15 basis points, and the 1H26 results drove the share price from A$0.94 to A$1.17 on five times average volume.

The evidence-weighted assessment is approximately A$1.02, roughly 13% below the current price of A$1.17. The gap reflects a disagreement about what is driving the company. The market is pricing a lender passing through a cyclical trough whose earnings will continue recovering. The evidence gives greater weight to a different reading: that the recovery is externally driven by rate cuts, and that underneath it the company is losing competitive ground, growing at one-sixth the sector rate, with no quantified strategy, governed by a 63.56% controlling shareholder who chairs the Audit Committee. The gap is directional but not definitive. Under plausible shifts in a single variable, it narrows to 2–4%.

The FY26 annual result, due August 2026, is the discriminating event. Two numbers matter. Organic home loan AUM growth, measured against PEXA sector data: above 8% and the competitive gap is closing, the assessment rises toward the current price; below 5% and the company is still losing share. Group net interest margin with the RBA on hold at 3.60%: above 150 basis points and the franchise retains margin without rate-cut support; below 145 basis points and the margin recovery was borrowed from the rate cycle.

Before August, the strategy disclosure promised for early 2026 will either provide quantified targets that make a turnaround credible or deliver another round of aspirational language. That distinction reshapes what is possible from here.

identity.overview: Resimac Group Limited (ASX: RMC) is a non-bank residential mortgage lender with a 40-year securitisation track record and A$50bn cumulative RMBS issuance since 1987. Assets under management of A$15.9bn are funded through limited-recourse securitisation trusts. The company is controlled by Duncan Saville (63.56% of issued capital via Somers Limited), who also chairs the Audit Committee. New CEO Pete Lirantzis was appointed April 2025 following executive turnover across CEO, CFO, and CIO roles. 1H26 showed a significant earnings inflection (EBIT +163%) driven primarily by RBA rate cuts improving NIM from 146bps to 161bps, with no top-line growth contribution. ASIC civil penalty proceedings for hardship compliance failures (January 2022 to February 2024) remain unresolved.

hypotheses.title: N1: Cyclical Trough, Durable Franchise
hypotheses.statusText: Priced: Recovery embedded in current P/B
hypotheses.description: 1H26 inflection marks the beginning of multi-period recovery. RBA rate cuts widen NIM; impairment moderates as credit cycle stabilises. Franchise durable with 40-year securitisation track record. ROE above cost of equity when macro cooperates. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$74M and ROE of 15.6%.
requires: RBA does not reverse course (existing cuts permanent)
Credit losses at or below 1H26 levels (annualised A$19M)
supporting: 1H26 inflection: EBIT +163% to A$39.9M, Group NIM +15bps to 161bps, impairment ↓34%
APRA DTI cap (Feb 2026): Redirects prime borrowers from ADIs to non-bank lenders, structurally expanding addressable market
40-year track record: A$50bn cumulative RMBS issuance since 1987; senior RMBS margins improving
Corporate debt fully repaid: Only limited-recourse securitisation debt remains
contradicting: Organic AUM growth 4% vs 25.3% sector (PEXA FY25, independently sourced)
Management attributes recovery to rate cuts, not operational improvement
Active broker count 1,897 still below historical levels; competitive distribution gap
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N1 is the only scenario where the franchise is intact and the cycle was the entire problem. The 5–6% organic growth assumption is above FY25's 4% but far below the 25% sector rate. Over three to five years, a lender growing at one-quarter of the sector rate becomes progressively less relevant to brokers, less able to support programmatic securitisation at current scale, and less attractive to the RMBS investor base.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: N1 assumes a franchise earning above cost of equity can sustain that performance while losing market share every period. The risk is that the franchise itself is the problem, and the cycle is merely providing a temporary reprieve. If FY26 organic AUM growth stays below 5% with the sector above 15%, N1 loses its most important requirement.

hypotheses.title: N2: Structurally Narrowing, Macro-Masked
hypotheses.description: 1H26 is the cyclical peak, not the recovery start. Competitive erosion masked by rate-driven NIM expansion. FY26 looks identical to N1 initially (EBIT A$69M); divergence emerges FY27 onward as rate tailwind fades. NIM compresses under competitive pressure; organic AUM growth stagnates while competitors grow at 6x the rate. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$13M and ROE of 3.6%.
requires: Competitive dynamics reassert once rate tailwind stabilises
NIM compresses as rate benefit fades under competitive pressure (161bps → 122bps by FY28)
Management fails to deliver strategy addressing scale disadvantage
supporting: Organic AUM grew 4% vs 25.3% sector (PEXA FY25): independently sourced, not management-selected
Pepper Money originations ~A$10.3bn (~2× Resimac's A$5.8bn); A$21.8bn AUM (37% larger)
Cost-to-income deteriorating: 48.8% → 53.6% vs Liberty at 28%; negative operating leverage (Opex +21% vs NII +7%)
Management attributes NIM recovery to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement. An admission that works against their interests, increasing credibility
Asset finance NIM fell 37bps post-Westpac acquisition; A$25.4M impairment = 112% of group total on 16% of loan assets
contradicting: APRA DTI cap (Feb 2026) could redirect sufficient volume to offset narrowing
NIM expansion of 15bps in 1H26 demonstrates rate sensitivity works in Resimac's favour
1H26 operating cash flow recovered from negative A$251M to positive A$205M
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: FY26 looks like N1. EBIT of A$69M captures residual rate-cut benefit and SG&A normalisation, producing a result that appears to confirm the cyclical recovery thesis. The divergence from N1 begins in FY27. By FY28, underlying ROE of 8.6% is below cost of equity, the same return environment that the market priced at 0.68–0.77× book for two years. The N2 earnings path is designed to be indistinguishable from N1 at the first observation point and divergent at the second.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The APRA DTI cap. If the volume of redirected borrowers is large enough, even a subscale operator like Resimac could capture sufficient volume to offset the competitive erosion. The DTI cap is a structural demand expansion that N2 acknowledges but underweights. It changes the size of the market rather than the allocation within it.

hypotheses.title: N3: Capital Extraction, Declining Franchise
hypotheses.description: Controlling shareholder's capital allocation is the organising principle. Cumulative dividends exceed cumulative net income by A$60M over three years. Equity eroding from A$365M toward A$290M. Related party lending escalating (A$8M FY24, A$31M FY25, A$25M 1H26). Distributions of A$161M at 4.7x annual earnings concurrent with 4% organic growth suggest extraction over reinvestment. Three-year path sees equity/assets ratio drop to 1.8%.
requires: Related party transactions continue at current escalating volumes
Management does not materially change capital allocation framework
ASIC proceedings result in material penalty (A$20–30M estimated range)
supporting: A$161M returned to shareholders across FY25–1H26 at 4.7× annual earnings
Related party lending escalated: A$8M (FY24) → A$31M (FY25) → A$25M (1H26) to Saville entities
Saville chairs Audit Committee that oversees the related party transactions he initiates; 3 of 5 directors compromised
No return-on-capital metric (ROE, ROIC, ROA) in STI or LTI compensation despite ROE being disclosed as company metric; AUM growth in both creates dilutive acquisition incentive
contradicting: Saville holds 63.56%: bears full economic consequences; 64 cents per dollar lost falls on his bloc
Corporate debt fully repaid; buyback reducing share count rather than offsetting dilution
Distinction between extraction and rational management of low-growth franchise depends on intent assessment
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: Dividends exceed net income in every year, and this is by design. N3 models the dividend as quasi-fixed, set by the controlling shareholder's extraction preference, with the equity base absorbing the residual. The FY28 one-offs (A$25M ASIC settlement, A$22M full goodwill impairment) are not surprises in this world. They are the crystallisation of risks that the capital allocation framework chose not to provision for.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The alignment argument. Saville holds 63.56% of the equity. Every dollar of lost market capitalisation costs him 64 cents. The strongest counter-argument: the controlling shareholder is not extracting value but rationally managing a company with limited reinvestment opportunities, returning surplus capital that would otherwise earn below cost of equity. The distinction between extraction and rational management of a low-growth franchise is one of intent, and intent cannot be directly observed from financial statements.

hypotheses.title: N4: Strategic Renewal Under New Leadership
hypotheses.description: New CEO's strategy delivers quantified targets with remuneration linkage. Controlling shareholder permits capital retention. Technology and broker investment produce competitive improvement. Three-year path delivers FY28 net income of A$92M and ROE of 19.6%. Requires fundamental shift in capital allocation that contradicts the current shareholder return programme of A$161M at 4.7x earnings.
requires: Controlling shareholder permits capital retention (A$37M+ per annum retained rather than distributed)
Strategy disclosure contains quantified targets (ROE, cost-to-income, organic AUM growth) with remuneration linkage
Organic home loan AUM growth accelerates to 10% (150% acceleration from current 4%)
Technology investment produces measurable competitive improvement (not "agentic AI automation" without benchmarks)
supporting: New CEO appointed April 2025 with mandate for strategic renewal; broad leadership refresh across operations, credit, technology, product, distribution
1H26 underlying ROE of 15.5% demonstrates earnings power when cycle cooperates
APRA DTI cap creates structural opportunity for non-bank lenders to capture redirected flow
contradicting: CEO 10 months into role with no quantified targets disclosed; 72% of forward guidance is directional or vague
Only 1 of ~14 forward statements is testable: Westpac auto portfolio A$12M operating profit (FY26)
A$161M distributed at 4.7× earnings concurrently with strategy development
Controlling shareholder veto: Saville receives 63.56% of every dollar distributed; all operational assumptions contingent on his consent to retain capital
hypotheses.distinguishingFeature: N4 is the only scenario where gross profit grows materially (A$165M to A$219M, up 33%) and the equity base expands rather than stagnates or erodes. The J-curve is visible: FY27 EBIT dips as the ASIC settlement hits, then FY28 surges to A$130M as strategic investment produces competitive improvement on a larger book.
hypotheses.uniqueVulnerability: The controlling shareholder veto. Every other condition is within the CEO's operational scope. Capital allocation is not. The decision to retain A$37M+ per annum requires Saville's affirmative consent, and Saville receives 63.56% of every dollar distributed. If Saville concludes that near-term distributions generate more personal value than uncertain franchise appreciation, the reinvestment pathway closes regardless of the strategy's merits. This is a governance risk that sits upstream of every operational assumption.

narrative.theNarrative: The market is pricing Resimac as a cyclical recovery story at 1.17× P/B, implying return on equity exceeding cost of equity on a sustained basis. The 1H26 result was genuinely impressive on the surface: EBIT +163%, NIM +15bps to 161bps, impairment ↓34%, operating cash flow swinging A$456M positive. But the entire recovery is cost-side and margin-side with no top-line growth contribution. Revenue grew just 2.4%. Management itself attributes the improvement to RBA rate cuts, not operational improvement, an admission against interest that strengthens the structural narrowing thesis. Organic home loan AUM grew 4% against 25.3% sector growth (PEXA, independently sourced). The competitive gap is widening: Pepper Money originations are ~2× Resimac's; cost-to-income is deteriorating while Liberty operates at 28%. Governance concentration (63.56% controlling shareholder chairing the Audit Committee), escalating related party transactions (A$8M → A$31M → A$25M), and ASIC civil proceedings add structural risk layers the market may be underpricing.

priceImplication.label: Embedded Assumptions at A$1.17
priceImplication.content: At 1.17× P/B, the price embeds approximately 48% probability on the cyclical recovery scenario (N1) vs the evidence-weighted 27%. The market is implicitly assuming: NIM sustained above 150bps without further rate cuts; organic AUM growth recovering toward sector averages; impairment staying at or below 1H26 annualised levels; ASIC proceedings resolving without material penalty; and controlling shareholder permitting sufficient capital retention for franchise investment. If organic AUM growth stays below 5% in FY26 while the sector grows >15%, the structural narrowing thesis (N2) gains 8–10 percentage points and the evidence-weighted price falls to A$0.88–0.92. If the August FY26 result shows organic growth >8% and NIM >150bps, the recovery thesis strengthens and the price likely tests A$1.23–1.24 resistance.

narrative.evidenceCheck: The strongest evidence domain is statutory filings (1H26 inflection is auditable) combined with independently sourced competitive data (PEXA organic AUM growth gap). Corporate communications are motivated but honest: management explicitly attributes the NIM recovery to rate cuts, not operational improvement. The governance evidence (controlling shareholder structure, related party escalation, compensation gaps) is objective and verifiable from annual reports. The weakest domains are forward guidance credibility (72% directional or vague) and strategic renewal probability (no quantified targets disclosed).
narrative.narrativeStability: LOW: Binary outcome driven by FY26 annual result in August 2026. The next 5 months will confirm whether 1H26 was a cyclical trough or a cyclical peak. High sensitivity to NIM (5bps change = ±A$8–9M NII = ±1.3–1.5ppt ROE). Thin equity buffer (2.4% of assets) amplifies both upside and downside. Strategy disclosure timing is an additional uncertainty.

qualityOfEarnings.inflectionDependency: Three converging factors drive the half-year improvement: NIM widened 15 basis points to 161bps as three RBA rate cuts flowed through the variable-rate book; impairment halved as credit stress moderated and FY25 write-off policy acceleration cleared stale receivables; and SG&A compressed. Revenue grew 2.4%. The entire profitability recovery is cost-side and margin-side, with no contribution from top-line growth. If the rate cycle pauses, impairment re-accelerates, or SG&A reverts, the improvement reverses on a flat revenue base.
qualityOfEarnings.threePhaseArc: The arc divides into three phases. FY22 was the peak: EBIT margin of 26.3% and ROE of 18.4%. FY23 through FY25 was sustained compression: EBIT fell from A$120M to A$44M as impairment expense rose tenfold, SG&A grew faster than revenue, and the home loan book contracted A$1.8bn before partially recovering. 1H26 is the inflection: EBIT margin expanded 460 basis points same-half year-on-year, impairment halved, and operating cash flow swung from negative A$251M to positive A$205M.
qualityOfEarnings.roeFlatterers: The FY25 ROE of 9.5% is almost entirely leverage-driven. EBIT margin contracted and asset turnover fell; the equity multiplier expanded from 36× to 46× because A$1.9bn in securitisation debt was added while equity declined A$51M through dividends exceeding earnings. The 1H26 normalised ROE of 15.5% is partly a denominator effect: equity fell from A$416M to A$365M through A$161M in capital returns. On a stable equity base, the ROE improvement would be materially smaller.
qualityOfEarnings.balanceSheetFragility: The buffer is thin. The A$25.4M asset finance impairment in FY25 alone consumed 6.4% of equity. If the accounting section's stress scenario materialised concurrently with the ASIC contingency, the combined A$35–49M impact would reduce equity by 8.8–12.3%.

evidence.intro: Ten evidence domains assessed across the epistemic hierarchy. Evidence ranked: Audited Statutory > Objective Data > Independent Sources > Consensus > Motivated > Noise.

cards.title: 1. Corporate Communications
cards.finding: 1H26 earnings beat driven by margin, not volume. EBIT +163% to A$39.9M with Group NIM widening 15bps to 161bps. Revenue grew just 2.4%. Management's five-point strategy (broker investment, asset finance reset, technology, product simplification, cost discipline) contains no quantified targets. Of ~14 forward statements, only the Westpac auto portfolio operating profit target (A$12M, FY26) is testable. Management explicitly attributes recovery to RBA rate cuts, a statement against interest that increases credibility but undermines the franchise thesis.
cards.tension: Management's honest attribution of recovery to rate cuts contradicts the market's franchise recovery pricing. The absence of quantified targets 10 months into the CEO's tenure is notable given the breadth of the announced strategy.
cards.source: RMC 1H26 Results Presentation, Feb 2026; FY25 Annual Report

cards.title: 2. Regulatory Filings & Financial Statements
cards.finding: 1H26 inflection is auditable and real, but equity base is thinning. ROE recovered to ~15.5% (annualised), but equity declined from A$416M to A$365M through A$161M in capital returns at 4.7× annual earnings. ECL provisioning shows directional conflict: Loss Given Default reduced 50bps during FY25 while Stage 3 balances rose 87% to A$200M and impairment surged 184%. Alternative reasonable ECL assumptions could produce provisions A$15–25M above reported, equal to 43–72% of statutory NPAT. Key audit matters flagged ECL model rebuild risk and goodwill impairment (market cap below book at two consecutive dates).
cards.tension: The improvement is mathematically real but partially manufactured by denominator effect (equity reduction) and ECL assumption choices that may not survive a prolonged downturn. Goodwill on HLLB CGU (A$21.7M) has negative headroom under stress.
cards.source: Resimac FY25 Annual Report (Deloitte audited); 1H26 Appendix 4D; Key Audit Matters

cards.title: 3. Broker & Consensus Estimates
cards.finding: Limited coverage reflects small-cap status. Sparse broker coverage for a A$463M market cap non-bank lender. Available estimates tend toward the cyclical recovery narrative (N1), anchoring on the 1H26 inflection without fully weighting the organic growth gap or governance structure. Consensus likely underweights the competitive erosion evident in independently sourced PEXA data.
cards.tension: The lack of sell-side coverage means the market may be pricing off limited analytical depth. Small-cap non-bank lenders attract minimal institutional research, creating information asymmetry that benefits the controlling shareholder.

cards.title: 4. Competitor & Industry Data
cards.finding: Organic growth gap is the single most diagnostic data point. Resimac organic home loan AUM grew 4% (FY25) vs non-ADI sector growth of 25.3% (PEXA). Pepper Money originations reached ~A$10.3bn (~2× Resimac's A$5.8bn) with AUM of A$21.8bn (37% larger). Liberty operates at 28% cost-to-income vs Resimac's 53.6%. Non-bank RMBS issuance rose to A$33.9bn (2024) from A$25bn (2023), indicating sector health that Resimac is not capturing proportionally.
cards.tension: The sector is growing rapidly, funding markets are supportive, and competitors are scaling, yet Resimac is growing at one-sixth the rate. This is the strongest evidence for structural narrowing and the hardest data point for the cyclical recovery thesis to explain.
cards.source: PEXA FY25 Mortgage Insights; Pepper Money FY25 Results; Liberty FY25 Results; APRA ADI Statistics

cards.title: 5. Economic & Regulatory Data
cards.finding: RBA rate cuts are the primary driver; APRA DTI cap is untested. Three RBA rate cuts of 25bps each through FY26 directly widened Resimac's NIM by 15bps to 161bps. The APRA DTI cap (effective February 2026) restricts ADI lending to borrowers with debt exceeding 6× income, potentially redirecting flow to non-bank lenders. However, Resimac's specific capture rate from DTI-redirected borrowers is unknown. RMBS senior margins have tightened, supporting funding costs.
cards.tension: Rate cuts help all non-bank lenders equally, not Resimac specifically. The DTI cap is structurally positive for the sector but may disproportionately benefit larger, better-distributed competitors (Pepper, Firstmac) who can capture redirected volume more effectively.
cards.source: RBA Official Cash Rate decisions; APRA DTI cap announcement Feb 2026; RMBS market data

cards.title: 6. Price Action & Market Behaviour
cards.finding: Market belief shifted sharply on 1H26 result. RMC rallied 18.9% in a single session on 5× average volume following the 1H26 result. The stock re-rated from below book value to a 17% premium. Prior to the result, the stock had recovered 55.7% from the July 2024 trough of A$0.79 to the October 2025 high of A$1.23, then consolidated around A$0.94–1.00 before the 1H26 catalyst. The single-day advance suggests the market was underweight the recovery scenario pre-result.
cards.tension: The magnitude of the single-day move (18.9%) suggests the market was caught underweight. But single-session re-ratings on earnings beats frequently overshoot, particularly in small-cap stocks with limited float. The 1.17× P/B pricing may be the market's first take, not its considered view.
cards.source: ASX market data; RMC price and volume history

cards.title: 7. Academic & Structural Research
cards.finding: Controlling shareholder literature flags minority expropriation risk. Academic research on concentrated ownership structures consistently finds that controlling shareholders above 50% can extract private benefits through related party transactions, preferential capital allocation, and information asymmetry. Non-bank lender structures funded through limited-recourse securitisation create additional agency risks as the corporate entity's equity buffer is thin relative to assets managed. RMBS market structure research shows non-bank lenders face pro-cyclical funding risk during market stress.
cards.tension: The academic framework supports heightened scrutiny of Saville's 63.56% control, but concentrated ownership also aligns incentives when the controlling shareholder bears majority economic loss. The empirical evidence suggests both value creation and extraction occur in controlled companies, depending on governance quality.
cards.source: Corporate governance literature; RMBS market structure research

cards.title: 8. Media & Public Commentary
cards.finding: ASIC proceedings generate periodic negative coverage. The ASIC civil penalty proceedings for hardship compliance failures (January 2022 to February 2024) have attracted media attention. Broader non-bank sector coverage tends to focus on the DTI cap opportunity and RMBS market conditions. No material insider activity or unusual corporate actions detected beyond the established capital return programme.
cards.tension: Media coverage amplifies the governance narrative but adds limited analytical value beyond the statutory evidence. The ASIC proceedings are factual; the risk is quantification (no contingent liability recognised, estimated exposure A$20–30M from public precedent).

cards.title: 9. Leadership & Governance Assessment
cards.finding: Governance concentration is structural, not transitional. Duncan Saville (63.56% via Somers Limited) chairs the Audit Committee that oversees related party transactions he initiates. Board composition: 3 compromised directors, 2 independent. Wayne Spanner chairs the Board, Remuneration & Nomination, and Risk committees simultaneously. Executive turnover across CEO, CFO, and CIO in FY25 creates execution risk. No return-on-capital metric (ROE, ROIC, ROA) exists in STI or LTI compensation despite ROE being disclosed as a company metric. AUM growth targets in both STI and LTI create dilutive acquisition incentive.
cards.tension: The governance structure is unusual but not illegal. Saville's majority ownership means he bears 64% of any value destruction, creating natural alignment. The question is whether the capital return programme (A$161M at 4.7x earnings) reflects rational management of a low-growth franchise or minority expropriation through excessive distributions.
cards.source: Resimac FY25 Annual Report; Related Party Disclosures; Remuneration Report

cards.title: 10. Ownership & Capital Flows
cards.finding: Capital allocation reveals priorities. Somers Limited (Saville) holds 63.56% of issued capital. Capital returns of A$161M across FY25–1H26 equate to 4.7× annual earnings, with ~A$102M flowing to Saville's bloc. Equity declined from A$416M to A$365M while operating in a growing market. The company simultaneously distributed capital and referenced "agentic AI automation" as a strategic initiative without quantified investment, timeline, or benchmarks. Related party lending to Saville entities escalated from A$8M (FY24) to A$31M (FY25) to A$25M (1H26).
cards.tension: High payout ratios in a low-growth franchise can be rational (returning excess capital). The question is whether A$161M at 4.7x earnings is excess capital or equity erosion. The concurrent absence of reinvestment in competitive position (organic AUM growing at one-sixth the sector rate) suggests the latter.
cards.source: ASX substantial holder notices; Resimac FY25 Annual Report; Appendix 4D

discriminators.intro: Four data points carry high diagnosticity for discriminating between Resimac's competing hypotheses.

rows.evidence: FY26 organic home loan AUM growth vs PEXA sector data. The single most diagnostic metric. Organic growth above 8% validates the franchise thesis (N1); below 5% while the sector grows above 15% confirms structural narrowing (N2).
Group NIM with RBA on hold at 3.60%. NIM above 150bps with rates stable indicates franchise margin power. NIM below 145bps indicates rate-driven reprieve now fading under competitive pressure.
rows.currentReading: 161bps at 1H26 with 75bps cumulative cuts: directionally ambiguous

rows.evidence: Strategy disclosure with quantified targets and remuneration linkage. Quantified targets with interim milestones activate N4 (Strategic Renewal). Qualitative iteration forecloses N4 and redistributes weight to N2 and N3.
rows.currentReading: 72% of guidance directional or vague; 1 of ~14 statements testable

rows.evidence: Capital return rate vs earnings power (FY26 dividend and buyback programme). Returns exceeding earnings confirm extraction trajectory (N3). Retention shift confirms renewal potential (N4).
rows.currentReading: A$161M distributed at 4.7× FY25 NPAT: extraction pattern

discriminators.nonDiscriminating: Broad ASX financials sector performance • General interest rate expectations • Non-bank sector sentiment indices • RMBS spread movements (affect all non-bank lenders equally and do not specifically discriminate between Resimac's competing hypotheses).

tripwires.intro: Three catalysts that would trigger reassessment of hypothesis weights, with specific thresholds defining positive and negative outcomes.

cards.name: FY26 Annual Result: The Definitive Test

conditions.if: If organic home loan AUM growth >8%, Group NIM >150bps with RBA on hold, and full-year impairment <A$18M
conditions.then: N1 (Cyclical Trough) gains 8–10 percentage points to 35–37%. Evidence-weighted price rises to A$1.14–1.18. Stock likely tests A$1.23–1.24 resistance. Gap closes; alignment with market pricing.

conditions.if: If organic AUM growth <5% with sector >15%, NIM <145bps, or impairment >A$22M
conditions.then: N2 (Structural Narrowing) gains 8–10 percentage points to 48–50%. Evidence-weighted price falls to A$0.88–0.92. Gap widens to 20–25%. Market reprices from 1.17× P/B toward 0.80–0.90×.

cards.source: Source: Resimac ASX FY26 Annual Report and Appendix 4E

cards.name: Strategy Disclosure: Quantified or Qualitative?

conditions.if: If strategy includes quantified targets (ROE, cost-to-income, organic AUM growth) with interim milestones and remuneration linkage
conditions.then: N4 (Strategic Renewal) gains 5–8 percentage points. Evidence-weighted price rises A$0.08–0.12. Provides first tangible evidence of franchise investment.

conditions.if: If strategy is a qualitative iteration with no measurable targets or remuneration linkage
conditions.then: N4 (Strategic Renewal) foreclosed as viable. Weight redistributes proportionally to N2 and N3. Evidence-weighted price falls A$0.03–0.05.

cards.source: Source: Company strategy disclosure; investor presentations

cards.name: ASIC Proceedings Outcome

conditions.if: If settlement or minor penalty (<A$5M) with standard compliance undertakings
conditions.then: Removes contingent A$20–30M liability overhang. Marginal positive for all scenarios. N3 (Capital Extraction) loses ~2 percentage points.

conditions.if: If material penalty (A$20–30M) with ongoing compliance obligations and increased operational costs
conditions.then: Crystallises 58–87% of one year's NPAT. Strengthens N3 (Capital Extraction). Evidence-weighted price falls A$0.05–0.08.

cards.source: Source: ASIC civil penalty proceedings; Federal Court of Australia

couldntAssess: Market share trend data: No time-series competitive positioning analysis available; reliant on single-period PEXA comparison
Broker metrics: No retention, attrition, or concentration risk data disclosed
Westpac auto portfolio: Post-acquisition integration performance and ECL coverage not separately disclosed
Technology capability: "Agentic AI automation" referenced with no quantified investment, timeline, or benchmarks
Organic growth attribution: No disclosure of which products, channels, or geographies drove the 4% growth
ECL sensitivity: No disclosure of impact from scenario weight shifts or LGD reversal to prior assumptions
gaps.analyticalLimitations: Resimac is a small-cap non-bank lender with limited broker coverage and institutional ownership. Information asymmetry is significant: the controlling shareholder possesses information advantages unavailable to minority holders. ECL provisioning relies on discretionary assumptions that could materially affect reported earnings (alternative reasonable assumptions produce provisions A$15–25M above reported, equal to 43–72% of statutory NPAT). The August 2026 FY26 result is the definitive discriminator between competing hypotheses.

crossover.description: 50-day MA crossed above 200-day MA following the 18.9% single-day advance on 1H26 results. Price has pushed above both averages but the crossover is extremely recent and unconfirmed.

trend.structure: Extended base formation from July 2024 (A$0.79 low) to February 2026, punctuated by a sharp rally from A$0.94 to A$1.17 over 5 trading days following the 1H26 result. Prior to the result, the stock had been in a gradual uptrend from the July 2024 trough, gaining 55.7% over 318 days to A$1.23 (October 2025) before consolidating. The 1H26 result triggered a single-day 18.9% advance on 5x average volume.

support.method: 50-day MA and prior consolidation zone around A$1.00-1.05

conditions: Organic home loan AUM growth accelerates above 8% in FY26
Group NIM sustains above 150bps with RBA on hold at 3.60%
ASIC proceedings resolve without material penalty (<A$5M)
Strategy disclosure contains quantified targets with remuneration linkage
NIM compresses below 145bps as rate-cut benefit fades under competitive pressure
Organic AUM growth stays below 5% while sector grows above 15%
Impairment re-accelerates as Westpac auto portfolio seasons beyond 12 months
ASIC penalty crystallises A$20–30M impact (58–87% of one year's NPAT)
ROE reverts below cost of equity; P/Book returns to 0.70–0.80×

riskAsymmetry.summary: The downside scenarios (N2 at A$0.69, N3 at A$0.47) carry 60% combined weight and sit 41–60% below the current price; the upside scenarios (N1 at A$1.41, N4 at A$2.05) carry 40% combined weight and sit 21–75% above it. The expected loss from the downside scenarios exceeds the expected gain from the upside.
riskAsymmetry.marketDisagreement: The evidence and the market disagree most on the probability of cyclical recovery. The market assigns approximately 48% to N1; the evidence assigns 27%. The mirror image: the evidence assigns 40% to N2; the market approximately 27%. This is a single disagreement expressed through two numbers: whether the 1H26 improvement is the beginning of a multi-period recovery or the peak of a rate-driven reprieve. One captures a moment; the other captures a structure.

conclusion.opening: The gap of 12.8% warrants attention but not conviction. It narrows to 2–4% under plausible perturbations and depends on a single disagreement about whether competitive erosion or cyclical recovery is the dominant force. The current price of A$1.17 sits 17% below N1's implied A$1.41, 70% above N2's implied A$0.69, and 43% below N4's A$2.05. It prices a world where the cyclical recovery thesis carries dominant weight and the structural risks identified across the specialist assessments carry residual weight.

paths.label: Path A
paths.outcome: N1 gains 8–10 percentage points, moving to ~35–37%. Evidence-weighted price shifts to A$1.14–1.18, aligning with the market and eliminating the downside gap. Stock likely tests A$1.23–1.24 resistance. Requires DTI cap to have delivered measurable volume to Resimac specifically.

paths.label: Path B
paths.outcome: N2 gains 8–10 percentage points, approaching 48–50%. Evidence-weighted price drops to A$0.88–0.92, widening the gap to 20–25%. Market reprices from 1.17× P/Book toward 0.80–0.90×. Confirms competitive pressure alone is sufficient to erode the margin recovery.

paths.label: Path C
paths.outcome: Ambiguous. Weight shifts modest (2–3 percentage points). Evidence-weighted price remains near A$1.02–1.08. Market holds near current levels. Strategy disclosure content becomes the tiebreaker: if quantified, N4 gains; if qualitative, the reverse.

conclusion.closing: This analysis cannot resolve whether the organic AUM growth gap of 4% versus 25.3% is a structural feature of a shrinking franchise or a transitional artefact of leadership change and deliberate credit tightening. That distinction is the difference between a stock that is modestly above fair value and one that is 21% below it.

hero.embedded_thesis: At A$1.17 (1.17x P/B), the price embeds a cyclical recovery narrative where 1H26's 163% EBIT improvement marks the beginning of multi-period earnings recovery. The valuation implies ROE sustaining above cost of equity, NIM remaining above 150bps, organic AUM growth recovering toward sector averages, and ASIC proceedings resolving without material impact. The price does not embed continued competitive erosion at the rate observed in FY25 (4% organic growth vs 25.3% sector).
hero.skew_description: Two downside hypotheses carry 60% combined weight. N2 (Structurally Narrowing) at 40% is the dominant hypothesis and rising as competitive data accumulates. N3 (Capital Extraction) at 20% is rising as governance evidence builds. N1 (Cyclical Trough) at 27% is priced in at the current 1.17x P/B. N4 (Strategic Renewal) at 13% has low probability with no quantified strategy disclosed.

position_in_range.note: Scenario-implied prices based on probability-weighted P/B multiples applied to projected book equity

next_decision_point.metric: Organic home loan AUM growth; group NIM; impairment expense; capital return programme
next_decision_point.thresholds: Organic AUM growth above 8% with NIM above 150bps on RBA hold confirms franchise resilience and validates N1 recovery. Organic AUM growth below 5% with NIM below 145bps confirms N2 structural narrowing and reprices toward 0.80-0.90x P/B.

footer.disclaimer: This report does not constitute personal financial advice. Continuum Intelligence synthesises cross-domain evidence using the Analysis of Competing Hypotheses (ACH) methodology. All factual claims are sourced from ASX filings, company disclosures, ASIC proceedings, PEXA market data, and publicly available information. This report does not contain buy, sell, or hold recommendations.
